1	package agent
2	
3	import "strings"
4	
5	// OperatorCancelReason marks a landing preserved because the operator
6	// cancelled the attempt mid-flight via /api/beads/<id>/cancel. ADR-022
7	// §Cancel SLA: the worker aborts at the next safe point (between LLM turns /
8	// git ops) and emits a preserved_for_review result with this exact reason.
9	const OperatorCancelReason = "operator_cancel"
10	
11	// PushFailedReasonPrefix is the canonical Reason prefix written by the
12	// landing layer when `git push` rejects the new tip after a local merge or
13	// fast-forward succeeded. ClassifyExecuteBeadStatus matches on this prefix
14	// to map a merged-locally-but-not-pushed outcome to push_failed instead of
15	// success, so the bead stays open.
16	const PushFailedReasonPrefix = "landed locally; push failed:"
17	
18	// PushConflictReasonPrefix marks a more specific failure mode than
19	// PushFailedReasonPrefix: the local push was rejected because the remote
20	// advanced, the loop attempted an automatic pull/merge to recover, and the
21	// merge produced a conflict the loop cannot resolve. The bead is parked for
22	// human review with a short cooldown rather than a year-long park.
23	const PushConflictReasonPrefix = "landed locally; push conflict:"
24	
25	// FailureMode constants classify *why* an execution did not land cleanly.
26	// They are orthogonal to Outcome/Status: a single failed attempt carries one
27	// outcome (e.g. preserved) and one failure mode (e.g. merge_conflict) so
28	// measurement surfaces can aggregate failures by cause.
29	const (
30		FailureModeContextOverflow                = "context_overflow"
31		FailureModeMergeConflict                  = "merge_conflict"
32		FailureModeTestFailure                    = "test_failure"
33		FailureModeBuildFailure                   = "build_failure"
34		FailureModeTimeout                        = "timeout"
35		FailureModeAuthError                      = "auth_error"
36		FailureModeNoChanges                      = "no_changes"
37		FailureModeNoEvidenceProduced             = "no_evidence_produced"
38		FailureModeRatchetMiss                    = "ratchet_miss"
39		FailureModeNoViableProvider               = "no_viable_provider"
40		FailureModeProviderConnectivity           = "provider_connectivity"
41		FailureModeHarnessNotInstalled            = "harness_not_installed"
42		FailureModeBlockedByPassthroughConstraint = "blocked_by_passthrough_constraint"
43		FailureModeAgentPowerUnsatisfied          = "agent_power_unsatisfied"
44		FailureModeLockContention                 = "lock_contention"
45		FailureModeWorktreeLost                   = "worktree_lost"
46		// FailureModeRouteResolutionTimeout classifies an attempt that was parked
47		// because route resolution / routing preflight exceeded the configured
48		// route-resolution timeout. The lease is released and the bead is flagged
49		// for operator attention rather than auto-retried (ddx-d8970a7b).
50		FailureModeRouteResolutionTimeout = "route_resolution_timeout"
51		// FailureModeProgressWatchdog classifies an attempt the progress watchdog
52		// terminated because phase-empty heartbeats (harness/model/route all empty)
53		// persisted past the phase budget. The worker appeared fresh to the
54		// liveness TTL while making no forward progress, so the lease is released
55		// and the bead is flagged for operator attention (ddx-dc23f001, parent
56		// ddx-8f2e0ebf criterion B).
57		FailureModeProgressWatchdog = "progress_watchdog"
58		// FailureModeConsecutiveWedge classifies a bead the consecutive-wedge guard
59		// stopped re-claiming because it wedged (route_resolution_timeout or
60		// progress_watchdog) on consecutive claims up to the threshold. The bead is
61		// parked to proposed for operator attention so the single-threaded worker
62		// keeps draining the rest of the queue instead of re-wedging the same bead
63		// indefinitely (ddx-9714eaac, parent ddx-8f2e0ebf criterion E).
64		FailureModeConsecutiveWedge = "consecutive_wedge"
65		// FailureModeAttemptIntegrity classifies an attempt that produced commits
66		// and a clean agent exit but failed DDx's structured post-agent integrity
67		// validation (post-commit mutation, empty pre-commit gate evidence, or
68		// uncommitted tracked changes after the implementation commit). It is
69		// distinct from implementation/agent failures so operators can tell a DDx
70		// validation rejection apart from a genuine task failure (ddx-725b65b4).
71		FailureModeAttemptIntegrity = "attempt_integrity"
72		FailureModeUnknown          = "unknown"
73	)
74	
75	// ResourceExhaustedStopMessage is the operator-visible message emitted when
76	// execution must stop because the host cannot safely continue draining the
77	// queue.
78	const ResourceExhaustedStopMessage = "resource exhausted after cleanup; stopping work loop"
79	
80	// ClassifyFailureMode derives a failure_mode for a worker-level result from
81	// its outcome, exit code, and error message. Returns "" when the outcome is
82	// task_succeeded (success carries no failure mode). task_no_changes always
83	// maps to FailureModeNoChanges regardless of error text.
84	//
85	// For task_failed (or non-zero exit code), the error message is scanned for
86	// well-known substrings. The order matters: context/auth/timeout patterns
87	// are checked before generic test/build patterns so a "test timed out"
88	// classifies as timeout rather than test_failure. Anything unrecognised
89	// maps to FailureModeUnknown so aggregates never hide a missed pattern.
90	func ClassifyFailureMode(outcome string, exitCode int, errMsg string) string {
91		switch outcome {
92		case ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskSucceeded:
93			if exitCode == 0 {
94				return ""
95			}
96			// Degenerate: success outcome with non-zero exit. Treat as unknown
97			// failure rather than success so the aggregate flags the anomaly.
98		case ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoChanges:
99			return FailureModeNoChanges
100		case ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoEvidence:
101			return FailureModeNoEvidenceProduced
102		}
103	
104		lower := strings.ToLower(errMsg)
105		switch {
106		case IsLockContentionError(errMsg):
107			return FailureModeLockContention
108		case IsWorktreeLostError(errMsg):
109			return FailureModeWorktreeLost
110		case containsAny(lower,
111			"passthrough constraint unsatisfiable",
112			"passthrough constraint:",
113			"max_power is less than min_power",
114			"max_power exceeds min_power",
115			"must be less than max_power",
116			"no viable routing candidate for pins",
117			"harness cannot satisfy power constraint",
118			"harness pin incompatible with power bounds",
119			"model pin incompatible with power bounds",
120			"provider pin incompatible with power bounds"):
121			return FailureModeBlockedByPassthroughConstraint
122		case containsAny(lower,
123			"agent power unsatisfied",
124			"no model meets min_power",
125			"no model with power >=",
126			"minimum power not achievable",
127			"min_power constraint cannot be satisfied"):
128			return FailureModeAgentPowerUnsatisfied
129		case containsAny(lower,
130			"executable file not found in $path",
131			"executable file not found",
132			"no such file or directory") &&
133			containsAny(lower, "exec:", "harness", "claude", "agent", "codex", "gemini"):
134			return FailureModeHarnessNotInstalled
135		case containsAny(lower,
136			"no viable harness",
137			"no harness configured",
138			"failed to initialize routing service",
139			"resolveroute:",
140			"no viable routing candidate",
141			"no live provider supports",
142			"no candidate satisfying local endpoint",
143			"no viable provider"):
144			return FailureModeNoViableProvider
145		case containsAny(lower,
146			"context length",
147			"context_length_exceeded",
148			"context window",
149			"maximum context",
150			"prompt is too long",
151			"prompt too long",
152			"token limit",
153			"too many tokens",
154			"exceeds the context"):
155			return FailureModeContextOverflow
156		case containsAny(lower,
157			"unauthorized",
158			"401 unauthorized",
159			"invalid api key",
160			"invalid_api_key",
161			"authentication failed",
162			"authentication_error",
163			"no api key",
164			"missing api key",
165			"quota exceeded",
166			"insufficient quota",
167			"insufficient_quota",
168			"rate limit",
169			"rate_limit",
170			"429"):
171			return FailureModeAuthError
172		case containsAny(lower,
173			"timeout",
174			"timed out",
175			"context deadline exceeded",
176			"deadline exceeded",
177			"signal: killed"):
178			return FailureModeTimeout
179		case containsAny(lower,
180			"merge conflict",
181			"ff-merge not possible",
182			"merge failed",
183			"automatic merge failed"):
184			return FailureModeMergeConflict
185		case containsAny(lower,
186			"build failed",
187			"compilation failed",
188			"compile error",
189			"compilation error",
190			"cannot find package",
191			"undefined:",
192			"undefined reference",
193			"syntax error"):
194			return FailureModeBuildFailure
195		case containsAny(lower,
196			"test failed",
197			"tests failed",
198			"--- fail:",
199			"assertion failed",
200			"test failure"):
201			return FailureModeTestFailure
202		}
203		if outcome == ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskFailed && errMsg != "" {
204			return FailureModeUnknown
205		}
206		if exitCode != 0 {
207			return FailureModeUnknown
208		}
209		return ""
210	}
211	
212	// IsWorktreeLostError reports whether errMsg describes the execute-bead
213	// infrastructure losing the isolated attempt worktree before DDx could inspect
214	// its HEAD. This deliberately requires both a HEAD/worktree signal and a
215	// missing-path signal so generic harness "no such file" errors do not get
216	// bucketed as infrastructure loss.
217	func IsWorktreeLostError(errMsg string) bool {
218		lower := strings.ToLower(errMsg)
219		hasHeadSignal := containsAny(lower,
220			"failed to read worktree head",
221			"git rev-parse head",
222			"worktree path missing")
223		hasMissingPathSignal := containsAny(lower,
224			"chdir",
225			"no such file or directory",
226			"cannot access",
227			"does not exist")
228		return hasHeadSignal && hasMissingPathSignal
229	}
230	
231	// containsAny reports whether s contains any of the given substrings. s is
232	// assumed to already be lower-cased by the caller.
233	func containsAny(s string, needles ...string) bool {
234		for _, n := range needles {
235			if n == "" {
236				continue
237			}
238			if strings.Contains(s, n) {
239				return true
240			}
241		}
242		return false
243	}
244	
245	// classifyLandingFailureMode computes the final failure_mode for a result
246	// after the orchestrator has made its landing decision. It prefers
247	// landing-specific signals (merge conflict, gate failure) over the worker's
248	// classification and falls back to workerMode for worker-level failures
249	// (agent execution failed, context overflow, etc.). Returns "" when the
250	// bead merged cleanly.
251	func classifyLandingFailureMode(landingOutcome, landingReason string, gateResults []GateCheckResult, workerMode string) string {
252		switch landingOutcome {
253		case "merged":
254			return ""
255		case "no-changes", ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoChanges:
256			return FailureModeNoChanges
257		case "no-evidence", ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoEvidence:
258			return FailureModeNoEvidenceProduced
259		case "error":
260			if workerMode != "" {
261				return workerMode
262			}
263			return FailureModeUnknown
264		case "preserved":
265			switch {
266			case landingReason == "merge conflict" || landingReason == "merge failed" || landingReason == "ff-merge not possible":
267				return FailureModeMergeConflict
268			case landingReason == "post-run checks failed", strings.HasPrefix(landingReason, PreMergeChecksReason):
269				return classifyGateFailure(gateResults)
270			case landingReason == RatchetPreserveReason:
271				return FailureModeRatchetMiss
272			case landingReason == AttemptIntegrityPreserveReason:
273				return FailureModeAttemptIntegrity
274			case landingReason == OperatorCancelReason:
275				return ""
276			}
277			// Other preserved reasons (e.g. "agent execution failed",
278			// "--no-merge specified") defer to the worker's classification.
279			if workerMode != "" {
280				return workerMode
281			}
282			return FailureModeUnknown
283		}
284		if workerMode != "" {
285			return workerMode
286		}
287		return FailureModeUnknown
288	}
289	
290	// classifyGateFailure inspects failed gate results to distinguish
291	// build_failure from test_failure. Gate definitions that fail to compile
292	// before running (go build, tsc, cargo build) typically surface build
293	// diagnostics in stderr; test runners surface test failure markers. When
294	// ambiguous, defaults to test_failure since the post-run checks gate is
295	// test-oriented by convention.
296	func classifyGateFailure(gateResults []GateCheckResult) string {
297		for _, gr := range gateResults {
298			if gr.Status != "fail" {
299				continue
300			}
301			combined := strings.ToLower(gr.Stdout + "\n" + gr.Stderr)
302			if containsAny(combined,
303				"build failed",
304				"compilation failed",
305				"compile error",
306				"compilation error",
307				"cannot find package",
308				"undefined:",
309				"undefined reference",
310				"syntax error") {
311				return FailureModeBuildFailure
312			}
313		}
314		return FailureModeTestFailure
315	}
316	
317	// Worker-level outcome constants — set by ExecuteBead() on the result.
318	// The parent orchestrator (LandBeadResult + ApplyLandingToResult) then
319	// overwrites Outcome and Status with the landing decision.
320	const (
321		ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskSucceeded  = "task_succeeded"
322		ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskFailed     = "task_failed"
323		ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoChanges  = "task_no_changes"
324		ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoEvidence = "task_no_evidence"
325	)
326	
327	// Status constants — used by the loop and other consumers of ExecuteBeadReport.
328	// These map to specific close/unclaim behaviors in ExecuteBeadWorker.
329	const (
330		ExecuteBeadStatusStructuralValidationFailed = "structural_validation_failed"
331		ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed            = "execution_failed"
332		ExecuteBeadStatusPostRunCheckFailed         = "post_run_check_failed"
333		ExecuteBeadStatusRatchetFailed              = "ratchet_failed"
334		ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflict               = "land_conflict"
335		ExecuteBeadStatusPushFailed                 = "push_failed"
336		ExecuteBeadStatusPushConflict               = "push_conflict"
337		ExecuteBeadStatusPreservedNeedsReview       = "preserved_needs_review"
338		ExecuteBeadStatusNoEvidenceProduced         = "no_evidence_produced"
339		ExecuteBeadStatusNoChanges                  = "no_changes"
340		ExecuteBeadStatusAlreadySatisfied           = "already_satisfied"
341		ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess                    = "success"
342		ExecuteBeadStatusResourceExhausted          = "resource_exhausted"
343	
344		// ExecuteBeadStatusDeclinedNeedsDecomposition is a structured outcome
345		// signalling that the executor inspected the bead and concluded it cannot
346		// be delivered in a single pass — typically because the scope is
347		// epic-sized and must first be broken into sub-beads. It is distinct
348		// from no_changes (which means "I tried and there was nothing to change")
349		// and from execution_failed (which means the agent or harness errored).
350		//
351		// The loop treats this status as terminal-for-loop: it parks the bead
352		// with a long cooldown and records the recommended sub-beads as a
353		// structured `decomposition-recommendation` event. No further attempts
354		// happen until an operator clears the cooldown (e.g. via
355		// `ddx bead cooldown clear`) or splits the bead.
356		ExecuteBeadStatusDeclinedNeedsDecomposition = "declined_needs_decomposition"
357	
358		// Post-merge review outcomes. The bead was merged, then reviewed;
359		// the review returned a non-APPROVE verdict and the bead was reopened.
360		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewRequestChanges = "review_request_changes"
361		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewBlock          = "review_block"
362		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewMalfunction    = "review_malfunction"
363	
364		// ExecuteBeadStatusReviewRequestClarification is set when the reviewer
365		// returns REQUEST_CLARIFICATION — it cannot adjudicate one or more
366		// needs-judgment AC items without operator input. The bead moves to
367		// status=proposed (operator lane) without blocking the drain queue.
368		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewRequestClarification = "review_request_clarification"
369	
370		// ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflictUnresolvable is set when work
371		// attempted 3-way ort auto-resolution and (if configured) a focused
372		// conflict-resolve agent run for a preserved iteration, and both failed.
373		// The bead is parked under LandConflictCooldown for operator review.
374		ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflictUnresolvable = "land_conflict_unresolvable"
375	
376		// ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflictOperatorRequired is set when the focused
377		// conflict-resolve agent escalated to BLOCKING — the conflict requires
378		// operator judgment, not automated retry. The bead moves to status=proposed
379		// with a kind:land-conflict-operator-required event.
380		ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflictOperatorRequired = "land_conflict_operator_required"
381	
382		// ExecuteBeadStatusReviewTerminalBlock is set when a BLOCK review verdict
383		// is classified as a terminal operator-required condition —
384		// review_spec_gap, review_missing_acceptance, review_too_large at depth
385		// cap, or review_unsafe_or_out_of_scope. These classes do not re-enter
386		// the automated retry cycle and do not consume the no-progress retry
387		// budget; the bead moves to status=proposed until an operator
388		// intervenes.
389		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewTerminalBlock = "review_terminal_block"
390	
391		// ExecuteBeadStatusReviewFixableGap is set when a BLOCK review verdict is
392		// classified as a fixable gap — the issues are addressable by a repair
393		// attempt at higher MinPower with the reviewer's rationale as context.
394		// Exactly one repair cycle is scheduled per result_rev; a second
395		// review_fixable_gap on the same result_rev falls through to the regular
396		// BLOCK triage path instead.
397		ExecuteBeadStatusReviewFixableGap = "review_fixable_gap"
398	
399		// ExecuteBeadStatusRepairCycleExhausted is set when the candidate-cycle
400		// repair loop reaches its per-attempt repair_max_cycles limit before a
401		// reviewer approves the candidate. The latest candidate ref is retained for
402		// operator inspection.
403		ExecuteBeadStatusRepairCycleExhausted = "repair-cycle-exhausted"
404	)
405	
406	// ClassifyExecuteBeadStatus maps a landing outcome to the supervisor-visible
407	// status contract. This is called by ApplyLandingToResult and by callers who
408	// build an ExecuteBeadReport from a landing result.
409	func ClassifyExecuteBeadStatus(outcome string, exitCode int, reason string) string {
410		if exitCode != 0 {
411			return ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
412		}
413	
414		switch outcome {
415		case "merged":
416			// A merged outcome whose reason carries the push-failure marker
417			// means the local target branch advanced but `git push` rejected the
418			// new tip (large blob, branch protection, auth, divergence, etc.).
419			// Classify these as push_failed so the loop refuses to close the
420			// bead and operators are forced to investigate.
421			if strings.HasPrefix(reason, PushConflictReasonPrefix) {
422				return ExecuteBeadStatusPushConflict
423			}
424			if strings.HasPrefix(reason, PushFailedReasonPrefix) {
425				return ExecuteBeadStatusPushFailed
426			}
427			return ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess
428		case "no-changes", ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoChanges:
429			return ExecuteBeadStatusNoChanges
430		case "no-evidence", ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskNoEvidence:
431			return ExecuteBeadStatusNoEvidenceProduced
432		case "error":
433			return ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
434		case "preserved":
435			switch {
436			case reason == "merge conflict" || reason == "merge failed" || reason == "ff-merge not possible":
437				return ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflict
438			case reason == "post-run checks failed", strings.HasPrefix(reason, PreMergeChecksReason):
439				return ExecuteBeadStatusPostRunCheckFailed
440			case reason == RatchetPreserveReason:
441				return ExecuteBeadStatusRatchetFailed
442			case reason == AttemptIntegrityPreserveReason:
443				return ExecuteBeadStatusPreservedNeedsReview
444			case reason == OperatorCancelReason:
445				return ExecuteBeadStatusPreservedNeedsReview
446			}
447			if isPreservedNeedsReviewReason(reason) {
448				return ExecuteBeadStatusPreservedNeedsReview
449			}
450			// Preserved iterations may still be success when the caller
451			// explicitly requested no merge.
452			return ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess
453		case ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskSucceeded:
454			return ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess
455		case ExecuteBeadOutcomeTaskFailed:
456			return ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
457		default:
458			return ExecuteBeadStatusExecutionFailed
459		}
460	}
461	
462	// IsResourceExhaustedStatus reports whether the status is the stable
463	// host-resource failure classification.
464	func IsResourceExhaustedStatus(status string) bool {
465		return status == ExecuteBeadStatusResourceExhausted
466	}
467	
468	func isPreservedNeedsReviewReason(reason string) bool {
469		for _, prefix := range []string{
470			"evidence commit failed:",
471			"large-deletion gate:",
472			"syntax sanity gate:",
473			"post-land gate failed:",
474		} {
475			if strings.HasPrefix(reason, prefix) {
476				return true
477			}
478		}
479		return false
480	}
481	
482	func ExecuteBeadStatusDetail(status, reason, errMsg string) string {
483		switch {
484		case reason != "" && errMsg != "" && reason != errMsg:
485			return reason + ": " + errMsg
486		case reason != "":
487			return reason
488		case errMsg != "":
489			return errMsg
490		case status != "":
491			return status
492		default:
493			return "unknown"
494		}
495	}

6	---
7	# Feature: Beads (Work Item Tracker)
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-004
10	**Status:** Complete
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	Beads are portable, ephemeral work items with metadata. The `ddx bead` command provides CRUD, dependency tracking, and JSONL interchange — giving AI agents and workflows a structured work queue without coupling to any specific methodology.
17	
18	The name follows the `bd` (Dolt-backed) and `br` (SQLite-backed) convention: short, lowercase tool names for the same conceptual unit. DDx beads use JSONL-backed local storage; `bd` and `br` provide database-backed alternatives. All three interchange via JSONL.
19	
20	## Problem Statement
21	
22	**Current situation:** AI agents need a work queue to know what to do next. HELIX built its own tracker (`scripts/tracker.sh`) tightly coupled to HELIX workflow semantics (phase labels, spec-id enforcement). Other workflows have no reusable work-tracking primitive.
23	
24	**Pain points:**
25	- No generic work-item tool — every workflow reinvents issue tracking
26	- HELIX tracker can't be used without HELIX's full label/validation requirements
27	- No portable format for carrying work items between projects and tools
28	- Agents can't discover ready work without workflow-specific logic
29	
30	**Desired outcome:** A lightweight, workflow-agnostic bead tracker that any methodology can layer semantics on top of. HELIX adds phase labels; a kanban workflow adds columns; a plain project just tracks tasks.
31	
32	## Requirements
33	
34	### Functional
35	
36	1. **Bead CRUD** (`ddx bead create/show/update/close`) — create, read, update, and close work items
37	2. **Listing and filtering** (`ddx bead list`) — filter by status, label, or custom predicates
38	3. **Dependency DAG** (`ddx bead dep add/remove/tree`) — declare ordering constraints between beads
39	4. **Ready queue** (`ddx bead ready`) — list `status=open` beads with all dependencies satisfied and no execution-suppressing metadata
40	5. **Dependency-waiting query** (`ddx bead blocked`) — list `status=open` beads with unsatisfied dependencies; this is derived waiting, not `status=blocked`
41	6. **Status summary** (`ddx bead status`) — counts persisted-status values and derived buckets such as ready and dependency-waiting
42	7. **Import** (`ddx bead import`) — ingest beads from `bd`, `br`, or raw JSONL files
43	8. **Export** (`ddx bead export`) — write beads as JSONL for interchange with other tools
44	9. **Initialization** (`ddx bead init`) — create storage file and directory
45	10. **Validation hooks** — workflows register custom validators for create/update operations
46	11. **Claim ownership** (`ddx bead update <id> --claim [--assignee A]`) — claim a bead with explicit assignee control and stable claim metadata
47	12. **Execution evidence** (`ddx bead evidence add/list`) — append-only history for close summaries, agent outputs, and experiment outcomes
48	13. **Unknown field preservation** — round-trip fields DDx doesn't know about (enables workflow-specific extensions)
49	14. **In-priority queue ordering override** (`ddx bead queue ...`) — let operators move a bead within its existing priority bucket without changing priority or extending the core bead schema
50	
51	### Non-Functional
52	
53	- **Performance:** All local operations complete in <100ms for up to 10,000 beads. Read-heavy queue commands (`list`, `ready`, `blocked`, `status`) operate on one parsed snapshot and remain deterministic under repeated calls.
54	- **Portability:** No external dependencies beyond the `ddx` binary
55	- **Concurrency:** File-level locking prevents corruption from parallel writes. Writers use temp-file + atomic rename semantics, and repair flows keep a backup before swapping in a cleaned file.
56	- **Recovery:** Malformed or partially written JSONL records are handled best-effort. Valid records are preserved, malformed lines are reported with line numbers, and a `.bak` backup is kept when the store auto-repairs a file.
57	- **Interchange:** JSONL format compatible with `bd`, `br`, and HELIX tracker
58	
59	## Bead Schema
60	
61	| Field | Type | Required | Default | Notes |
62	|-------|------|----------|---------|-------|
63	| id | string | auto-generated | `bx-` + 8 hex chars | Prefix configurable |
64	| title | string | yes | — | Non-empty |
65	| type | string | no | `task` | Free-form (task, epic, bug, chore, etc.) |
66	| status | string | no | `open` | Enum: open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled (bd/br canonical set; see TD-027 §1) |
67	| priority | int | no | 2 | Range 0-4 (0 = highest) |
68	| labels | []string | no | [] | Free-form, no enforcement |
69	| parent | string | no | "" | ID of parent bead |
70	| description | string | no | "" | |
71	| acceptance | string | no | "" | |
72	| deps | []string | no | [] | IDs of dependency beads |
73	| assignee | string | no | "" | |
74	| notes | string | no | "" | |
75	| created | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |
76	| updated | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |
77	
78	Unknown fields in imported or existing beads are preserved on read/write. This allows HELIX to store `spec-id`, `execution-eligible`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`, `superseded-by`, `replaces`, and DDx-specific queue metadata such as `queue-rank` without extending the core bd/br-compatible schema table.
79	
80	Operator-attention work uses `status=proposed` when a bead needs a human
81	decision before autonomous execution. Legacy/backcompat `needs_human` labels
82	and legacy/backcompat `needs-human-*` preserved `Extra` fields may be read
83	during migration, but they are metadata only; they do not define a queue lane or
84	suppress routing after the status-owned lifecycle contract in TD-027 is applied.
85	
86	TD-027 §3 (Category Taxonomy) defines how lifecycle actions use the existing carriers: persisted
87	bd/br statuses, labels, dependency edges, append-only events, and preserved
88	`Extra` fields. FEAT-004 does not introduce additional status vocabulary for
89	no_changes, cooldown, superseded, or execution-readiness cases.
90	
91	### Lifecycle Migration Gate
92	
93	The status-owned lifecycle migration is one-way. Legacy/backcompat
94	`needs_human`, `triage:needs-investigation`, and pseudo-status lifecycle names
95	are input to `ddx bead migrate --lifecycle`; normal runtime does not preserve
96	them as compatibility queue lanes.
97	
98	Before queue commands derive readiness or mutate beads, DDx performs the TD-027
99	lifecycle startup preflight. If the active store still contains unmigrated
100	legacy lifecycle labels or pseudo-statuses, `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead
101	blocked`, `ddx bead status`, `ddx work`, and worker/API queue-readiness surfaces
102	must refuse normal operation. The permitted bead commands during this state are
103	the migration path and diagnostics:
104	
105	```bash
106	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run
107	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply
108	```
109	
110	The refusal output must include counts by legacy label/pseudo-status, a sample
111	of affected bead IDs, and the exact migration command to run. Because beads are
112	git-tracked, recovery from an incorrect one-way migration is git rollback of
113	the tracker commit rather than maintaining dual lifecycle semantics.
114	
115	Bead readiness assessment uses those existing metadata carriers. Lint/rubric
116	scoring happens inside readiness, and post-attempt triage happens after
117	execution. None of them add dedicated readiness fields or new schema fields;
118	readiness is derived from the bead record's existing title, description,
119	acceptance, labels, parent, dependencies, claim metadata, and preserved
120	extras.
121	
122	### Queue Semantics For Epics
123	
124	Epics remain first-class beads, but they are not consumed by the same
125	queue-drain contract as ordinary executable task/bug/chore beads.
126	
127	- A normal execution-ready queue is **single-ticket-first**. Ready non-epic
128	  beads are ordered ahead of ready epic beads at the same priority.
129	- Open epics are not launched by the ordinary `ddx work` single-ticket worker.
130	  Their disposition is decided by the **idle-path auto-remediator** (see FEAT-010
131	  Layer 3 — Idle-Path Diagnosis and Auto-Remediation), which classifies each
132	  ready epic by its open/closed child counts, parent/dep state, and operator
133	  override labels, then fires exactly one of these auto-actions per loop pass:
134	  - **Closure cascade** when all children are closed (`openChildCount == 0 &&
135	    totalChildCount > 0`): the epic is treated as a closure candidate and the
136	    existing closure-evaluation path auto-closes it.
137	  - **Supersession cascade** when an open child carries `superseded-by:<Y>`
138	    and Y is closed, with no other reason for the child to stay open: the
139	    child auto-closes via a one-hop cascade inside `Close()`.
140	  - **Auto-decomposition** when the epic has `totalChildCount == 0`, has a
141	    valid decomposition source (description with PROBLEM/PROPOSED FIX/AC
142	    sections, `spec:*` or `area:*` label), and has not exhausted its
143	    per-bead attempt cap or `--max-recovery-cost` budget: the in-loop
144	    pre-claim decomposer is dispatched against the epic.
145	  - **Operator surface** when none of the above applies (real dependency
146	    block on open work, parent/child state conflict, exhausted attempts,
147	    operator-set `manual-hold` / `no-auto-decompose` / `container` label,
148	    or unknown state): the epic is listed in `ddx work focus` Section A
149	    (operator-required). Beads with an auto-action queued for the next loop
150	    pass are surfaced in `ddx work focus` Section B (count with `--verbose`
151	    detail) rather than silently hidden.
152	- Child beads of an epic remain individually executable units and may be
153	  closed one-by-one.
154	- Epic queue entries remain visible in the tracker and UI. The diagnostic
155	  surface `ddx work plan --explain` prints the per-epic reason code so
156	  operators can see what the loop will do on its next iteration.
157	
158	The "skip and exit" disposition (`No execution-eligible beads in the queue`
159	with only ready epics present) is forbidden by the FEAT-010 reliability
160	contract; every ready epic must classify into one of the four auto-actions
161	above. Concurrency, cycle guards, idempotency, and audit-event requirements
162	are owned by FEAT-010 Layer 3 and are common to every auto-remediation in the
163	idle path.
164	
165	**Deferred** (out of scope for this contract): an epic-scoped worker mode
166	with a dedicated epic branch/worktree that sequentially executes child beads
167	on a shared branch. That capability is a separate, larger initiative and is
168	not required for the queue-drain contract to hold.
169	
170	### Queue Ordering Overrides
171	
172	The canonical ready-queue order is:
173	
174	1. `priority` ascending (`0` first, `4` last)
175	2. explicit `queue-rank` ascending, with missing `queue-rank` sorted after
176	   explicit ranks inside the same priority bucket
177	3. `created_at` ascending
178	4. `id` ascending
179	
180	`queue-rank` is preserved extension metadata stored in the bead's unknown-field
181	map, not a core bead schema field. It is an operator override for ordering
182	within one priority bucket only. A ranked `P1` bead never sorts ahead of an
183	unranked `P0` bead, and a ranked bead that is blocked, on retry cooldown,
184	superseded, `execution-eligible=false`, or epic-only/container work remains
185	excluded from `ddx work`'s execution-ready picker.
186	
187	The CLI exposes queue movement as a first-class surface rather than requiring
188	operators to manage raw metadata:
189	
190	- `ddx bead queue top <id>` assigns a rank that places the bead first among
191	  ready beads with the same priority.
192	- `ddx bead queue move <id> --before <other-id>` places the bead before another
193	  bead in the same priority bucket.
194	- `ddx bead queue move <id> --after <other-id>` places the bead after another
195	  bead in the same priority bucket.
196	- `ddx bead queue clear <id>` removes the explicit rank and restores the
197	  default tie-break ordering for that bead.
198	
199	`queue move --before/--after` fails when the two beads have different
200	priorities. Operators that want to change urgency must use `ddx bead update
201	<id> --priority N` explicitly. Queue-rank values are canonicalized as integers;
202	read paths may accept numeric strings for compatibility, but writes persist a
203	number. Rank calculation should use sparse integer values and renormalize only
204	the affected priority bucket when no midpoint exists.
205	
206	## Storage
207	
208	DDx beads abstracts over multiple storage backends. Consumers (HELIX, dun, other workflows) **must only interact via `ddx bead` commands or the server API** — never read/write the storage file directly.
209	
210	### Backend Selection
211	
212	| Backend | Config Value | When To Use |
213	|---------|-------------|-------------|
214	| `jsonl` (default) | `bead.backend: jsonl` | Simple projects, single-agent, no external tooling |
215	| `bd` | `bead.backend: bd` | Dolt-backed bead store (collaborative, branching) |
216	| `br` | `bead.backend: br` | SQLite-backed bead store (local performance) |
217	
218	Backend is configured in `.ddx/config.yaml` or via `DDX_BEAD_BACKEND` env var. Default: `jsonl`.
219	
220	For `bd` and `br` backends, DDx shells out to the respective binary. For `jsonl`, DDx manages the file directly.
221	
222	### JSONL Backend Details
223	
224	- **Default path:** `.ddx/beads.jsonl` (one JSON object per line, sorted by id)
225	- **Configuration:** `DDX_BEAD_DIR` env var or `bead.dir` in `.ddx/config.yaml` (default: `.ddx`)
226	- **Locking:** Directory-based lock at `.ddx/beads.lock/` with PID file and acquisition timestamp. Configurable timeout (default: 10s).
227	- **Write algorithm:** Mutating operations take the lock, build a complete bead snapshot, write to `beads.jsonl.tmp`, and atomically rename the temp file into place. This avoids partially written tracker state.
228	- **Read algorithm:** Queue commands scan `beads.jsonl` line-by-line. Valid JSON objects are loaded into a snapshot; malformed lines are skipped with line-numbered warnings so one bad record does not take down the entire queue.
229	- **Repair algorithm:** If a read finds malformed lines but at least one valid bead, DDx takes the store lock, copies the current file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak`, and rewrites the repaired snapshot atomically.
230	- **Failure mode:** If every line is malformed, the command returns a contextual error that names the file and malformed-record count.
231	- **Git integration:** `beads.jsonl` is project state and must be committed to git. Auto-commit behavior after bead mutations is defined in FEAT-012 (Git Awareness), requirements S7 and US-124. `ddx init` generates agent guidance that includes `beads.jsonl` as a committable file (FEAT-012 S8, US-125).
232	- **Design reference:** See [`SD-004-beads-tracker.md`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-004-beads-tracker.md), [`TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md), and [`TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../03-test/test-plans/TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md) for the concrete algorithms, repair flow, claim semantics, evidence trail, and validation matrix.
233	
234	### Storage Boundary
235	
236	The bead backend is the reusable DDx storage engine for bead-schema records.
237	The primary work queue is one collection, not the only possible collection.
238	
239	- The default active-work collection maps to `beads.jsonl` in the JSONL backend.
240	- Other DDx services may use separate bead-backed collections for archived
241	  work, execution history, agent session indexes, or other record families.
242	- Large payloads such as prompt bodies or raw logs live in separate attachment
243	  files referenced from the bead-schema record rather than inline in the
244	  primary collection row.
245	- Backend selection is a store-level concern, not a single-file concern.
246	
247	### ID Prefix
248	
249	Following the bd convention, the prefix is **derived from the repository name** by default:
250	
251	- **Auto-detected:** git repo root directory name (e.g., repo `my-project` → prefix `my-project` → IDs like `my-project-a3f2dd`)
252	- **Override:** `bead.id_prefix` in `.ddx/config.yaml` or `DDX_BEAD_PREFIX` env var
253	- **Fallback:** `bx` if repo name detection fails
254	- **Format:** `<prefix>-` + 8 hex chars
255	
256	This matches how bd derives its prefix, ensuring beads created by DDx and bd in the same repo use the same naming convention.
257	
258	## Validation
259	
260	**DDx base validation** (always applied):
261	- Title is non-empty
262	- Dependencies reference existing bead IDs
263	- No self-referential dependencies
264	- Priority is in valid range (0-4)
265	- Status is a recognized value
266	
267	**Workflow validation hooks:** An executable at `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-create` (and `validate-bead-update`) receives the bead JSON on stdin. Exit codes: 0 = ok, 1 = hard error (stderr = message, creation blocked), 2 = warning (stderr = message, creation proceeds).
268	
269	### Bead Readiness And Authoring Quality
270	
271	ADR-023 adds lifecycle-quality hooks on top of the existing validation-hook
272	surface. The canonical product concept is bead readiness assessment: the
273	pre-claim decision about whether a bead is tractable and actionable. The same
274	policy surface also includes lint/rubric scoring inside readiness, which
275	measures prompt quality, and post-attempt triage, which classifies evidence
276	after execution. The older "pre-claim intake" wording survives only as legacy
277	compatibility language, not normative terminology. Base validation still
278	protects the reusable bead schema; authoring quality checks protect the
279	"bead as prompt" contract needed by `ddx try`, `ddx work`, and autonomous
280	sub-agent execution. Readiness uses the existing bead metadata carriers and
281	does not add schema fields.
282	
283	The lint rubric is the 8-criterion template in
284	`docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`:
285	
286	- title is scoped and imperative
287	- description contains problem, root cause with file:line, proposed fix, and
288	  non-scope
289	- acceptance criteria are numbered, verifiable, and name concrete tests or
290	  observable artifacts when tests apply
291	- new code paths include wired-in assertions
292	- acceptance criteria name the applicable `go test` command and
293	  `lefthook run pre-commit`
294	- labels include phase, area, kind, and cross-references
295	- parent and dependencies are explicit
296	- the bead body is sufficient for a competent sub-agent to execute without
297	  asking for operator context
298	
299	Bead readiness assessment, lint/rubric scoring, and post-attempt triage all
300	invoke the nested bead-lifecycle skill under the `ddx` skill tree. DDx owns

data.output:
300	invoke the nested bead-lifecycle skill under the `ddx` skill tree. DDx owns
301	passing bead JSON, mode, waiver labels, and evidence paths into the hook; the
302	skill owns producing human-readable criterion findings. Hook output is
303	ephemeral execution evidence, not durable bead schema. For `ddx try` and
304	`ddx work`, the readiness and lint reports are stored under the attempt
305	evidence directory alongside the prompt, result, checks, and triage records.
306	`beads.jsonl` is not extended with lint-score fields.
307	
308	Waiver storage uses existing labels. The durable form is
309	`lint-waiver:<criterion>`, such as `lint-waiver:c` for the concrete-test-name
310	criterion. Built-in rubric skips come from the authoring template: doc-only
311	beads may skip test-name and wired-in criteria, epic beads may satisfy those
312	criteria through children, and deletion/rename beads may skip wired-in checks
313	when behavior preservation is asserted.
314	
315	Manual override is explicit and audited. When an operator dispatches with
316	`--force --reason <text>`, DDx appends an evidence event recording the actor,
317	reason, mode, waived criteria, and lint summary. The override does not mutate
318	the bead schema and does not suppress future lint runs.
319	
320	WARN-ONLY is the default mode: lint findings are reported but create/update and
321	dispatch proceed. BLOCK mode is opt-in and may block dispatch only after valid
322	lint output, rubric skips, and label waivers have been applied. Hook
323	infrastructure failures follow ADR-023's fail-open rule and are reported as
324	warnings, not schema validation failures.
325	
326	## CLI Surface
327	
328	```
329	ddx bead init
330	ddx bead create "Title" [--type T] [--priority N] [--labels L,L] [--acceptance A] [--parent ID] [--description D]
331	ddx bead show <id> [--json]
332	ddx bead update <id> [--title T] [--status S] [--priority N] [--labels L,L] [--acceptance A] [--assignee A] [--claim]
333	ddx bead queue top <id>
334	ddx bead queue move <id> [--before OTHER | --after OTHER]
335	ddx bead queue clear <id>
336	ddx bead evidence add <id> [--kind K] [--body B] [--summary S] [--source SRC] [--actor A]
337	ddx bead evidence list <id> [--json]
338	ddx bead close <id>
339	ddx bead list [--status S] [--label L] [--json]
340	ddx bead ready [--json]
341	ddx bead blocked [--json]
342	ddx bead status
343	ddx bead dep add <id> <dep-id>
344	ddx bead dep remove <id> <dep-id>
345	ddx bead dep tree [<id>]
346	ddx bead import [--from auto|bd|br|jsonl] [file]
347	ddx bead export [--stdout] [file]
348	```
349	
350	## Server Endpoints (FEAT-002 integration)
351	
352	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description |
353	|----------|--------------|-------------|
354	| `ddx_list_beads` | `GET /api/beads` | List beads with optional filters |
355	| `ddx_show_bead` | `GET /api/beads/:id` | Show one bead |
356	| `ddx_bead_ready` | `GET /api/beads/ready` | List ready beads |
357	| `ddx_bead_status` | `GET /api/beads/status` | Summary counts |
358	
359	## User Stories
360	
361	### US-020: Create and Manage Beads
362	**As a** developer tracking work items
363	**I want** to create, update, and close beads from the CLI
364	**So that** I have a lightweight work queue without external tools
365	
366	**Acceptance Criteria:**
367	- Given I'm in a DDx project, when I run `ddx bead create "Fix auth bug" --type bug --priority 1`, then a bead is created with a unique ID and status `open`
368	- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update <id> --status in_progress --assignee me`, then the bead is updated
369	- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead close <id>`, then its status is set to `closed`
370	- Given beads exist, when I run `ddx bead list --status open`, then only open beads are shown
371	
372	### US-021: Track Dependencies Between Beads
373	**As a** developer with ordered work
374	**I want** to declare that bead A must complete before bead B
375	**So that** the ready queue reflects actual execution order
376	
377	**Acceptance Criteria:**
378	- Given beads A and B exist, when I run `ddx bead dep add B A`, then B depends on A
379	- Given B depends on open A, when I run `ddx bead ready`, then B is not listed
380	- Given B depends on A and A is closed, when I run `ddx bead ready`, then B is listed
381	- Given I run `ddx bead dep tree`, then I see the full dependency graph
382	
383	### US-022: Import Beads from External Sources
384	**As a** developer migrating from another tracker
385	**I want** to import beads from `bd`, `br`, or JSONL files
386	**So that** I can consolidate work items into DDx
387	
388	**Acceptance Criteria:**
389	- Given a `.beads/issues.jsonl` file exists, when I run `ddx bead import --from jsonl`, then beads are imported preserving all fields
390	- Given `bd` is available and has data, when I run `ddx bead import --from bd`, then beads are imported
391	- Given I run `ddx bead export --stdout`, then JSONL output is compatible with `ddx bead import --from jsonl`
392	
393	### US-023: Agent Queries Ready Beads via MCP
394	**As an** AI agent with MCP tool access
395	**I want** to query the ready queue programmatically
396	**So that** I can pick up the next actionable work item
397	
398	**Acceptance Criteria:**
399	- Given ddx-server is running with beads, when an agent calls `ddx_bead_ready`, then it receives ready beads as structured JSON
400	- Given an agent calls `ddx_show_bead` with an ID, then it receives the full bead including all fields (known and unknown)
401	
402	### US-023a: Operator Reorders Work Within Priority
403	**As an** operator managing the ready queue
404	**I want** to move a bead ahead of other beads with the same priority
405	**So that** I can express immediate sequence without changing urgency or rewriting the core bead schema
406	
407	**Acceptance Criteria:**
408	- Given two ready `P0` beads exist, when I run `ddx bead queue top <later-id>`, then `ddx bead ready --execution` lists `<later-id>` before the other `P0` bead
409	- Given a `P1` bead has `queue-rank=0` and a `P0` bead has no `queue-rank`, when I run `ddx bead ready --execution`, then the `P0` bead remains before the `P1` bead
410	- Given two beads with different priorities, when I run `ddx bead queue move <id> --before <other-id>`, then DDx returns an error explaining that queue moves are limited to one priority bucket
411	- Given a ranked bead is blocked, superseded, on retry cooldown, `execution-eligible=false`, or an epic-only/container bead, then `ddx bead ready --execution` still excludes it
412	- Given a ranked bead exists, when I run `ddx bead queue clear <id>`, then DDx removes `queue-rank` and the bead returns to the default priority/created/id ordering
413	
414	### US-024: Operator Recovers From Partial JSONL Corruption
415	**As a** repo operator
416	**I want** bead queue commands to survive a partially written or externally edited `beads.jsonl`
417	**So that** one bad record does not block the whole queue
418	
419	**Acceptance Criteria:**
420	- Given `beads.jsonl` contains one malformed record and one valid record, when I run `ddx bead ready` or `ddx bead status`, then DDx reports the malformed line number, preserves the valid record, and rewrites the cleaned file atomically
421	- Given DDx auto-repairs a partially corrupted file, then it keeps a `.bak` backup of the original contents
422	- Given `beads.jsonl` contains only malformed records, then queue reads fail with a contextual error that includes the file path and malformed-record count
423	
424	### US-025: Operator Claims a Bead With a Specific Assignee
425	**As a** developer or agent coordinating work
426	**I want** to claim a bead and assign it to a known actor
427	**So that** ownership is explicit and not hardcoded to DDx
428	
429	**Acceptance Criteria:**
430	- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update <id> --claim --assignee alice`, then the bead becomes `in_progress`, `assignee` is `alice`, and `claimed-at` / `claimed-pid` are recorded
431	- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update <id> --claim` without `--assignee`, then DDx uses the runtime caller identity fallback before defaulting to `ddx`
432	- Given a claimed bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update <id> --unclaim`, then claim metadata is cleared and the bead returns to `open`
433	
434	### US-026: Operator Appends Execution Evidence
435	**As a** developer or agent
436	**I want** to append evidence to a bead without rewriting prior history
437	**So that** close summaries and experiment outcomes remain auditable
438	
439	**Acceptance Criteria:**
440	- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead evidence add <id> --kind summary --body "done"`, then a new immutable event is appended with a timestamp and actor
441	- Given evidence already exists, when I append another event, then prior entries remain in order and are not rewritten
442	- Given I run `ddx bead show <id> --json`, then the full evidence history is returned
443	- Given I run `ddx bead list`, `ready`, `blocked`, or `status`, then evidence entries do not change queue semantics
444	
445	## Claim Semantics
446	
447	Beads support advisory ownership claims for agent/workflow coordination:
448	
449	- `ddx bead update <id> --claim [--assignee NAME]` sets `status=in_progress`, resolves `assignee` from the explicit flag or runtime caller identity, and records `claimed-at` (ISO-8601 UTC) plus `claimed-pid` (current PID)
450	- `ddx bead update <id> --unclaim` sets `status=open`, clears `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`
451	- Claims are advisory — they prevent double-claiming, not hard locks
452	- `claimed-at` and `claimed-pid` are standard fields (not unknown-field extensions)
453	
454	Claim resolution order is:
455	
456	1. Explicit `--assignee` flag.
457	2. Runtime caller identity from the execution context.
458	3. `ddx` as the final fallback.
459	
460	This makes claim ownership explicit for both human operators and agents without hardcoding the caller to the CLI binary name.
461	
462	## Execution Evidence
463	
464	Beads carry append-only execution evidence in workflow-specific metadata at `Extra["events"]`.
465	
466	- Each event records `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`, `created_at`, and `source`.
467	- `kind` covers common workflow events such as `claim`, `unclaim`, `close`, `summary`, and `experiment`.
468	- Events are appended in order and never rewritten or removed by normal CLI operations.
469	- Evidence is visible in `show --json` and server/API responses via the `events` metadata field, but queue derivation ignores it.
470	- Workflows may use the evidence trail for operator notes, experiment results, or close summaries without changing the bead's core status semantics.
471	
472	## Custom Fields
473	
474	Workflows need to store fields DDx doesn't know about (e.g., HELIX stores `spec-id`, `execution-eligible`, `superseded-by`).
475	
476	- `ddx bead create --set key=value` — set arbitrary field on create
477	- `ddx bead update <id> --set key=value` — set arbitrary field on update
478	- `ddx bead update <id> --set closing_commit_sha=<sha>` — repair closing provenance on a closed bead without editing storage directly
479	- `ddx bead list --where key=value` — filter by custom field
480	- Unknown fields are preserved on all read/write operations
481	- Custom fields appear in JSON output and import/export
482	- The evidence trail is stored as workflow-specific metadata in `Extra["events"]`, preserving bd/br compatibility while still round-tripping the full history.
483	
484	This keeps DDx agnostic while giving workflows a typed pass-through mechanism.
485	
486	## Validation Hooks
487	
488	Workflows register custom validators as executables:
489	
490	- `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-create` — called on create, receives bead JSON on stdin
491	- `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-update` — called on update, receives bead JSON on stdin
492	- Exit 0 = ok, Exit 1 = hard error (creation blocked, stderr = message), Exit 2 = warning (proceeds, stderr = message)
493	
494	Example: HELIX installs a hook requiring `spec-id` on task-type beads and `acceptance` on task/epic beads.
495	
496	## Edge Cases
497	
498	- Create when storage doesn't exist — auto-initialize on first write
499	- Import with duplicate IDs — skip duplicates, report count
500	- Dep add with nonexistent target — error with clear message
501	- Concurrent writes — locking prevents corruption, second writer waits or times out
502	- Partially corrupted JSONL — valid records are preserved, malformed lines are skipped with contextual warnings, and auto-repair keeps a `.bak` backup before atomic replacement
503	- Export when no beads exist — produce empty JSONL file
504	- Unknown fields in imported data — preserve on round-trip without validation
505	
506	## Dependencies
507	
508	- `.ddx/` directory (created by `ddx init` or `ddx bead init`)
509	- `bd` / `br` only required for `--from bd` / `--from br` import
510	
511	## Out of Scope
512	
513	- Workflow-specific validation (phase labels, spec-id) — that's HELIX or other workflows via hooks
514	- Supervisory execution loops — that's workflow-level orchestration
515	- Real-time sync between bead stores — use import/export for interchange
516	- Web UI for bead management — use CLI or server API
517

1	<execute-bead>
2	  <instructions>
3	You are executing one bead in an isolated DDx execution worktree. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> are the contract: every AC must be provably satisfied by a specific code, test, or file after your commit.

## Step 0: size check

Too big if any holds:

- More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems.
- AC mixes design, implementation, integration tests, and docs.
- Description names multiple feature-sized pieces.
- More than ~500 lines across ~5+ files in unrelated packages.
- If the bead description exceeds 8000 bytes, use a split-first pass.
- Auto-decomposition is capped at depth 2: root beads may split once, children once more; reject third-level splits with an explanation.

If too big, decompose:

1. `ddx bead create` for each child (copy parent's labels and spec-id).
2. `ddx bead dep add <child-id> <parent-id>` to record edges.
3. `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
4. Write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path with child IDs, then stop.

Decomposition alone is success. Do not mix it with implementation.

## How to work

- Read first. If the bead names files, specs, or prior beads, read them before editing — do not guess.
- Cross-reference each AC to concrete evidence (test, file, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Run git/index mutations sequentially; do not use parallel tool calls for `git add`, `git commit`, or other staging/commit commands.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- If `lefthook run pre-commit` depends on staged files, rerun it after staging the exact commit set. A `no-staged-files` run is not acceptance evidence.
- Commit exactly once when green; conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- Current-bead lifecycle is orchestrator-owned. Do not run `ddx bead update <bead-id> --claim`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --status <status>`, `ddx bead update <bead-id> --unclaim`, or `ddx bead close <bead-id>`. Step 0 still allows `ddx bead create`, `ddx bead dep add`, and `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'`.
- If you cannot finish, write `no_changes_rationale.txt` under the bead metadata `bundle` path before exiting. No commit or rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`.

## no_changes contract

The `no_changes_rationale.txt` file under the bead metadata `bundle` path must contain one of:

- `verification_command: <cmd>` — repo cwd; exit 0 closes, nonzero rejects.
- `status: open` + `reason: <retryable>` — open, smart retry.
- `status: proposed` + `reason: <operator needed>` — operator lane.
- `status: blocked` + `reason: <external blocker>` — blocked lane.

No pseudo-statuses. Bare rationales and `status: needs_investigation` are rejected.

For orchestrator decomposition, add `orchestrator_action: decompose` alongside `status: open`.

## Reports

Reports go under the bead metadata `bundle` path in `.ddx/executions/`. **Never write reports to `/tmp` or outside the repo**. Use a named in-repo path when given; otherwise write `<short-name>.md` there. Commit it with the code.

## Mode and overrides

DDX_MODE=bead_execution: edit code/docs for bead AC. Only broad queue-steward default is overridden; tracker, merge-policy, verification, safety stay active. Bead description/AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and defaults.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other instruction files unless the bead asks.

## Long-running matrix/benchmark beads

For expensive commands (benchmarks, load tests, validation > 60s per variant):

- Write a matrix plan before launching expensive commands: list required configs, output paths, completion criteria.
- Do not re-run the same long-running command unless: (1) the command fingerprint changed (args/env/config), AND (2) you document why prior output is invalid and what changed.
- If a long-running command times out or is incomplete, exit with `status: open` + retryable `reason` in `no_changes_rationale.txt`. Do not silently retry; let the orchestrator decide.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator.
4	  </instructions>
5	  <bead id="ddx-8d6af771">
6	    <title>routing P1: ddx applies shared failure-classification table; single-owner one-hop middle->max escalation</title>
7	    <description>
8	Implement the ddx side of the FEAT-004 failure-classification table: ddx work maps each attempt outcome to exactly the action in the addendum table (success/already_satisfied=close; no_changes=close/unclaim NO tier-escalation; genuine impl/capability failure=escalate middle->max ONCE; dirty=clean+same-tier retry; transport=same-tier reroute; quota-exhausted=global pool cooldown; auth/setup=operator-attention; timeout=clean+retry then operator-attention; merge-conflict=unclaim+retry; max genuine failure=operator-attention). ddx owns the ONE middle->max tier escalation (per the escalation-owner decision bead fizeau-994604ec); a per-pool in-flight max-attempt cap prevents multi-worker stampede. Depends on the escalation-owner contract + the shared classifier (fizeau-44e98499).
9	    </description>
10	    <acceptance>
11	1. ddx classifies each outcome per the addendum table (table-driven test). 2. Exactly one middle->max escalation, only on typed genuine failure; no_changes/dirty/transport/quota/auth do NOT escalate. 3. Per-pool in-flight max-attempt cap enforced (test simulates concurrent genuine failures, asserts cap). 4. quota-exhausted -> global pool cooldown, not N per-bead mutations (test). 5. cd /Users/erik/Projects/ddx/cli && go test ./internal/agent/... green. 6. lefthook run pre-commit passes.
12	    </acceptance>
13	    <labels>area:execute-bead, area:workers, kind:feature, spec:FEAT-004, phase:1</labels>

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-010
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	    - FEAT-005
7	    - FEAT-006
8	---
9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge). Treat `internal/agent` as legacy naming only; new DDx-owned execution
34	work should make the workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Agent Interaction Modes
41	
42	DDx defines four named agent interaction modes (`DDX_MODE`) to separate
43	broad interactive queue stewardship from worker execution. The mode governs
44	what an agent opened in a DDx project is permitted to do.
45	
46	| DDX_MODE | Description | Allowed actions | Prohibited actions |
47	|---|---|---|---|
48	| `queue_steward` | Default interactive mode. Surveys, triages, and advises on the queue without claiming or executing beads. | Read tracker and docs; report status; advise on bead quality; suggest readiness fixes; run `ddx bead list/ready/status/show`. | Claiming beads; creating isolated workspaces; starting `ddx work` or `ddx try` without an explicit user directive. |
49	| `bead_execution` | Worker mode. Executes one or more beads from the queue in isolated workspaces. | Full FEAT-010 layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle: workspace creation, bead attempt, merge or preserve, evidence bundle. | Scope creep outside the named bead; switching to direct implementation without a bead. |
50	| `direct_user_implementation` | Human-directed implementation. The user has explicitly asked the agent to edit code or docs, bypassing the bead queue. | Edit code and docs as instructed; commit to the current branch. | Starting autonomous queue drain; claiming beads autonomously. |
51	| `review` | Read-only review. Grades an existing implementation against AC or specs. | Read code, docs, and evidence; return a structured verdict with per-AC findings. | Writing commits; claiming beads; modifying the tracker. |
52	
53	### Precedence
54	
55	`bead_execution` supersedes `queue_steward` when DDx invokes a worker
56	explicitly (via `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, or an internal execute-bead
57	worker invocation).
58	`direct_user_implementation` supersedes `queue_steward` when the user
59	explicitly asks for code or doc edits unrelated to the queue. `review`
60	supersedes `queue_steward` when the user explicitly requests a graded review
61	pass.
62	
63	Tracker instructions, merge-policy, and safety rules in AGENTS.md are
64	load-bearing in every mode and are never overridden by mode selection.
65	
66	### Lifecycle Migration Startup Gate
67	
68	`ddx work`, `ddx try`, server-managed workers, GraphQL worker starts, REST/MCP
69	worker starts, and queue status/readiness commands must run the TD-027 §18 lifecycle
70	startup preflight before claiming beads or deriving worker eligibility. When the
71	active queue still contains unmigrated legacy lifecycle labels or
72	pseudo-statuses, these surfaces fail closed with a configuration/startup error.
73	They do not call Fizeau, do not classify the failure as an agent attempt, and do
74	not add cooldown or retry metadata.
75	
76	Allowed bypass commands are limited to help/version output, `ddx doctor` or
77	other read-only diagnostics that do not mutate lifecycle, and the lifecycle
78	migration command:
79	
80	```bash
81	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run
82	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply
83	```
84	
85	The error must print counts of legacy labels and pseudo-statuses, a small sample
86	of affected bead IDs, and the exact migration command. The migration is one-way:
87	old `needs_human` and `triage:needs-investigation` lanes are not runtime
88	compatibility modes. If the migration is wrong, the recovery path is git
89	rollback of the tracker commit.
90	
91	### Worker-capacity suggestion rules
92	
93	An agent in `queue_steward` mode may suggest running `ddx work` or point
94	the operator to worker-capacity settings when:
95	
96	- The queue contains execution-ready beads that have not been attempted.
97	- The queue is blocked and operator action is required.
98	- The operator asks "why isn't anything running?" or a similar capacity question.
99	
100	The agent must not autonomously start `ddx work` from `queue_steward` mode.
101	Suggesting the command or explaining the conditions is the correct action.
102	
103	## Problem Statement
104	
105	**Current situation:** DDx has accreted two independent run-storage shapes
106	— `.ddx/exec-runs/` for generic execution evidence and
107	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` for tracked bead-attempt bundles. Loop behavior,
108	worktree lifecycle, and bead resolution are spread across legacy command
109	surfaces without a clean layering story. There is no single place to introspect "what runs
110	happened, at what layer, with what evidence."
111	
112	**Pain points:**
113	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
114	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
115	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
116	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
117	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
118	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
119	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
120	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
121	
122	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
123	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
124	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
125	
126	## Task Execution Layers
127	
128	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
129	
130	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
131	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
132	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
133	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
134	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
135	
136	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
137	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
138	provenance capture.
139	
140	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
141	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
142	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
143	
144	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
145	
146	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated workspace. It owns:
147	
148	- Workspace creation from a base revision and workspace finalization
149	  (merge or preserve)
150	- Workspace cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
151	  ordinary interruption
152	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
153	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
154	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
155	
156	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
157	
158	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
159	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
160	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
161	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
162	decision.
163	
164	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies the outcome → state
165	mapping in [`TD-031 §2`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md)
166	when that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
167	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
168	
169	### Quality hooks
170	
171	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
172	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
173	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
174	
175	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
176	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
177	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
178	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
179	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
180	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
181	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
182	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
183	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
184	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
185	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
186	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
187	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
188	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
189	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
190	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
191	layer-3 run record.
192	
193	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
194	paths. Decomposition decisions request Fizeau's `smart` model-ref and run with a
195	strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to the smart/top-power class floor when no
196	project-specific splitter override is configured. DDx does not choose the
197	concrete model; it passes the raised power floor to Fizeau and preserves any
198	operator-supplied harness/provider/model passthrough constraints. If those
199	constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor, DDx records readiness as
200	unavailable (`readiness_error` / `intake_error`) instead of treating the bead as
201	ambiguous or in need of human review; the worker then continues through the
202	normal implementation route with the same operator passthrough constraints.
203	
204	The readiness assessment result is one of:
205	
206	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
207	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied a validated replacement rewrite or
208	  metadata update that makes the bead more execution-ready before claim. Safe
209	  rewrites may replace noisy, stale, duplicated, or brittle description text
210	  with a fit-for-purpose bead body instead of appending more prompt burden. They may
211	  also format the description into the authoring template, add discovered
212	  file:line evidence, add an obvious test command, or wire deterministic
213	  labels/parent/deps. The mutation is recorded through `ddx bead` paths before
214	  claim, with before/after hashes and enough evidence to audit the replacement.
215	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child, sibling, or replacement beads,
216	  mapped every parent AC to generated ACs or an explicit operator-required /
217	  `non_scope` marker, left the oversized bead `status=open` with dependency,
218	  execution-eligibility, or supersession metadata that lets the queue advance,
219	  and did not execute the oversized bead directly.
220	- `ambiguous_requires_operator` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
221	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
222	  DDx moves the bead to `status=proposed` and does not claim it.
223	- `readiness_error` / `intake_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In
224	  migration/WARN mode this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK
225	  mode it may skip the candidate for the current pass but must not park it
226	  behind a cooldown unless a retryable time-based class is recorded.
227	
228	Safe rewrites are validated against durable context, not against a vague
229	"same spirit" comparison to the prior bead text. The prior bead can itself be
230	the problem: it may contain stale line numbers, duplicated history, chat-shaped
231	noise, or contradictory wording. A safe replacement must preserve explicit
232	commitments that remain valid, especially acceptance criteria, non-scope, named
233	files/tests, dependencies, and governing artifact references; it must either
234	carry forward durable root-cause evidence or replace it with current file:line
235	evidence from the repository or governing artifact. The original text is kept in
236	readiness evidence, not copied into the replacement body. Rewrites must optimize
237	prompt fitness rather than raw length: expanding a one-line underspecified bead
238	is safe when the added durable context is required for execution, and compressing
239	a noisy bead is safe when explicit commitments remain preserved.
240	
241	Readiness must reject the rewrite when preservation cannot be proven from
242	durable anchors. Rejected rewrite does not by itself authorize operator
243	attention: DDx next tries safe decomposition or replacement work when that can
244	preserve explicit scope, and moves the bead to `status=proposed` only when the
245	remaining ambiguity, missing governing artifact, or scope conflict requires
246	operator judgment. It must not invent product behavior, choose between
247	conflicting requirements, change scope, delete unresolved constraints, or guess
248	a missing governing artifact.
249	
250	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
251	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
252	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
253	report.
254	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
255	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
256	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
257	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
258	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
259	retry/stop classification.
260	
261	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
262	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
263	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
264	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
265	work: DDx files child, sibling, or replacement beads, records the AC map, and
266	leaves the oversized bead open with dependency, execution-eligibility, or
267	supersession metadata that lets the queue advance. Only lossy or ambiguous
268	splits require operator attention.
269	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
270	from inside the attempted execution.
271	
272	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
273	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
274	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
275	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
276	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
277	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
278	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
279	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
280	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
281	
282	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
283	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
284	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
285	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
286	
287	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
288	
289	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
290	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
291	
292	- Queue iteration order
293	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
294	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
295	  TD-027 §12 claim-state contract
296	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031 §2 outcome,
297	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
298	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
299	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
300	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
